source_ranking: Installed local SDK docs and headers from the user's machine or repo
Official NVIDIA developer pages, release notes, SDK guides, and official GitHub repositories
Official engine documentation and NVIDIA engine integration pages
NVIDIA Developer Forums as dated troubleshooting context only
Observed project source code and build files as the final arbiter
policy.download_guard: Never download SDKs or credential-gated assets automatically. Ask the user first and prefer official NVIDIA download pages.
policy.redistribution_guard: Do not redistribute NVIDIA SDK binaries or package them unless the user has confirmed license permission.

sources.name: NVIDIA DLSS Developer Page
sources.summary: Official NVIDIA DLSS page covering DLSS 4.5, Dynamic Multi Frame Generation, Super Resolution, Frame Generation, Ray Reconstruction, DLAA, Streamline, Unreal, and Unity routes.

sources.name: NVIDIA Streamline Developer Page
sources.summary: Official Streamline overview and route for integrating NVIDIA and related rendering features.

sources.name: NVIDIA-RTX Streamline GitHub Releases
sources.summary: Official Streamline releases. Streamline SDK 2.11.1 was listed on April 21, 2026 with Dynamic Multi Frame Generation Mode.

sources.name: Streamline Programming Guide
sources.summary: Official Streamline integration, security, plugin loading, distribution, and validation guidance.

sources.name: Streamline DLSS Frame Generation Programming Guide
sources.summary: Official DLSS Frame Generation integration checklist, required inputs, feature state, and present-time behavior.

sources.name: RTX Video SDK Getting Started
sources.summary: Official RTX Video SDK page. Lists Super Resolution, Artifact Reduction, SDR to HDR, RTX 50 support, native CUDA support, 10-bit video SR, and Windows/API/GPU requirements.

sources.name: NVIDIA Video Codec SDK
sources.summary: Official Video Codec SDK page covering NVENC/NVDEC hardware encode/decode, Windows/Linux support, C APIs, wrappers, DirectX, Vulkan Video, PyNvVideoCodec, and Blackwell updates.

sources.name: NVIDIA Reflex SDK
sources.summary: Official Reflex page covering Low Latency mode, Frame Warp, latency markers, Streamline integration, and C++ SDK route.

sources.name: NVIDIA Nsight Graphics
sources.summary: Official Nsight Graphics page for frame debugging, profiling, shader/pixel/event inspection, and graphics capture workflows.

sources.name: Nsight Graphics 2025.2 Release Page
sources.summary: Official release page describing Graphics Capture features and capture/replay workflows.

sources.name: NVIDIA RTX Kit
sources.summary: Official RTX Kit page covering neural rendering, neural shaders, neural texture compression, texture filtering, texture streaming, mega geometry, path tracing, and character rendering.

sources.name: WebGPU Explainer
sources.summary: WebGPU explains browser GPU access through web abstractions and does not promise vendor-specific native SDK exposure.

sources.name: NVIDIA Optical Flow SDK
sources.summary: Official NVIDIA Optical Flow SDK page. Describes NVOFA hardware, real-time interpolation/extrapolation, and NvOFFRUC frame-rate up-conversion for video playback smoothness.

sources.name: NVIDIA Optical Flow SDK 5.0 Documentation
sources.summary: Official Optical Flow SDK 5.0 documentation index with NVOF API, application note, tracker, and FRUC programming guide.

sources.name: NVOFA FRUC Programming Guide
sources.summary: Official FRUC guide. Explains frame-rate up-conversion by inserting interpolated frames between consecutive frames using NVOFA and CUDA.

sources.name: NVIDIA Optical Flow SDK Download Page
sources.summary: Official Optical Flow SDK download page with OS, driver, CUDA, compiler, CMake, and license-gated download requirements.

requirements: Real-time rendered frames, not arbitrary compressed video frames
Renderer must provide feature-specific resources such as color, depth, motion vectors, exposure, jitter, camera data, frame index, and reset state where required
Feature support must be queried through SDK or plugin state for the user's GPU, OS, driver, API, settings, and SDK version
Use production NVIDIA-signed binaries for shipping where official guidance requires it
Validate Streamline DLL signatures and use absolute paths when verifying signed modules
validation: Confirm feature support before exposing UI
Validate tagged resources and states
Use Streamline debug visualization where available
Confirm frame generation is disabled during pause, loading, menus, resolution changes, and non-gameplay frames
Validate latency and frame pacing with Reflex and profiling tools
Verify production/non-watermarked libraries before packaging

technologies.canonical_name: Official NVIDIA DLSS plugin for Unreal Engine
requirements: Detected Unreal Engine project and engine version
Match official plugin package to UE version
Validate plugin placement, project configuration, runtime logs, packaging, and production binaries
validation: Inspect .uproject/.uplugin state
Check configured DLSS/Reflex options
Validate in editor and packaged builds
Inspect logs and runtime feature availability

technologies.canonical_name: Unity HDRP DLSS and Reflex route
requirements: Unity HDRP route should be first-class for supported Unity versions
URP/custom SRP requires advanced render-pipeline inspection before feasibility claims
validation: Inspect Packages/manifest.json and HDRP configuration
Check render pipeline, camera, dynamic resolution, and runtime feature settings
Use Reflex measurement markers where latency matters

technologies.canonical_name: NVIDIA Optical Flow SDK / NvOFFRUC frame-rate up-conversion
requirements: Use for frame-rate up-conversion by generating interpolated frames between consecutive frames; do not confuse with DLSS Frame Generation for rendered game frames.
Requires NVIDIA Optical Flow SDK / NVOFA access and a native or backend layer; pure browser YouTube pages cannot directly call this SDK.
Official FRUC docs describe ARGB and NV12 input surface support and DirectX or CUDA application integration paths.
Official docs list Windows and Linux support with NVIDIA display driver and CUDA/toolchain requirements that must be checked for the installed SDK version.
For YouTube/browser playback, route through Electron/native companion, native player/plugin, or server-side pipeline rather than browser-only script injection.
validation: Confirm SDK version, driver, CUDA/toolchain, OS, and NVOFA-capable NVIDIA GPU before implementation.
Validate input frame format, timing, cadence, and consecutive-frame ordering.
Compare 60 fps input to 120 fps output for motion artifacts, duplicate frames, judder, and A/V sync.
Measure end-to-end latency, frame queue depth, dropped frames, and GPU/NVOFA/CUDA utilization.
For streamed content, validate buffering behavior and avoid violating platform terms or DRM boundaries.

requirements: 64-bit Windows 10 or newer
API route such as DX11, DX12, Vulkan, or CUDA
Supported NVIDIA RTX GPU, with official page listing GeForce RTX 20 series or newer and NVIDIA RTX 1000-class hardware
Use for Super Resolution, Artifact Reduction, and SDR-to-HDR video enhancement
validation: Test Super Resolution, Artifact Reduction, and SDR-to-HDR separately
Validate 8-bit and 10-bit inputs where relevant
Validate HDR10 output and display path where used
Measure real-time playback latency and throughput
Compare multiple source bitrates and resolutions

technologies.canonical_name: NVIDIA Video Codec SDK, NVENC, and NVDEC
requirements: Use NVENC for hardware encode and NVDEC for hardware decode
Do not claim CUDA cores perform NVENC/NVDEC work; these are dedicated hardware engines
Check GPU codec support matrix for codec, bit depth, chroma format, and throughput
Use FFmpeg/GStreamer when the project already depends on those frameworks, or low-level APIs when granular control is required
validation: Check codec support on the selected GPU
Test encode/decode throughput
Validate profile, level, rate-control mode, color format, bit depth, and latency
Measure quality with PSNR, SSIM, VMAF, or project-specific metrics
Validate audio/video sync and zero-copy/GPU-memory claims
Profile NVENC/NVDEC utilization separately from CUDA/graphics workload

requirements: Use Reflex for latency-sensitive games and frame generation workflows
Implement measurement markers for debugging and visualization where appropriate
Use Streamline route when it fits the project; use Reflex SDK route when Streamline is not suitable
validation: Measure click-to-photon or pipeline latency
Validate frame pacing with and without Frame Generation
Check CPU/GPU bound scenarios

technologies.canonical_name: Nsight Graphics, Nsight Systems, and Nsight Aftermath
requirements: Use frame debugging and Graphics Capture for rendering state and resource inspection
Use GPU Trace or Nsight Systems for performance and frame pacing analysis
Use Aftermath-style crash dump planning for D3D12/Vulkan GPU hangs and TDRs
validation: Define exact repro scene and capture mode
Collect frame captures, traces, or crash dumps as appropriate
Inspect API state, events, objects, resources, shaders, acceleration structures, and GPU timeline

technologies.canonical_name: NVIDIA RTX Kit and neural rendering technologies
requirements: Treat RTX Kit technologies as renderer/content-pipeline technologies, not DLSS substitutes
Select individual RTX Kit components based on asset, material, geometry, denoising, path tracing, or character-rendering goals
validation: Validate visual quality and performance per component
Use Nsight for renderer profiling and shader/resource investigation
Confirm GPU/driver/API requirements from the exact component repository or SDK

technologies.canonical_name: Browser, WebGPU, WebCodecs, and native NVIDIA boundary
requirements: Do not claim pure browser apps can directly call native DLSS, RTX Video SDK, or Video Codec SDK APIs unless official web-accessible support exists
Route pure web apps to WebGPU/WebCodecs/browser-native paths
Route NVIDIA SDK needs to Electron/native companion, native app/plugin architecture, or server-side NVIDIA GPU pipeline
validation: Define browser-only versus native/backend boundary
Specify IPC, frame sharing, texture sharing, latency, and process lifetime if using a native companion
